data.title: STMicroelectronics: DC And Robotics Opportunities Re-Ignited Enthusiasm (Upgrade)
data.description:
The near-term outlook for key semiconductor customers in the auto and industrial markets is still murky, but exposure to growth opportunities in data centers, sensing, robotics, and power continues to resonate well with investors. To that end, STMicroelectronics’ (STM) recent efforts to highlight its leverage to these markets, as well as growing confidence that the worst is behind the business, have boosted sentiment for these shares.

STMicro shares are up about 20% since my last update, a strong result relative to the broader semiconductor space, as well as more direct comparables like Infineon (IFNNY), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), ON Semiconductor (ON), and Renesas Electronics (RNECY).

Provided that the ongoing hostilities between the United States, Israel, and Iran don’t trigger a broader downturn for the economy, STMicro’s business should recover in 2026, with notable acceleration in the second half of the year continuing into 2027 and beyond. Moreover, while STMicro does face meaningful competition across multiple markets, opportunities in areas like data centers (or DCs), power, robotics, and sensing do support a more bullish outlook.

A Quick Look Back

Expectations for STMicro’s fourth quarter were modest, and the company basically met them.

Revenue was basically flat from the prior year and up more than 4% from the prior quarter, which was good for a small beat relative to the Street. While the auto business remains pressured (down 15% yoy and up 3% qoq), personal electronics, industrial, and communications markets were all stronger.

Gross margin declined 250bp from the prior year as excess capacity continues to weigh, but did improve about two points sequentially on a better mix. Operating income declined 28% yoy and rose 23% qoq, with margin down about three points yoy to 8%, with pronounced weakness in its auto-driven Power & Discrete Products driving the weakness.

Inventory days improved by 5 to 130 in the fourth quarter, and management expects excess inventory to be worked through by the end of the second quarter. That said, order visibility in the auto market is still problematic, and while industrial demand has improved, it’s coming up from a very low base. More positively, the company continues to gain content with Apple (AAPL) as well as with low-earth orbit satellite manufacturers.

New Content Wins, Recovering Power, And DC Opportunities Driving A Better Revenue Outlook

With the auto sector still accounting for around 40% of demand, this is clearly still an important market for STMicro and arguably the one with the most uncertain outlook today. Overall vehicle production growth is expected to be low in 2026, but semiconductor content continues to grow, particularly in areas like ADAS and controls.

STMicro’s leverage and opportunity here are complicated. While electric vehicle demand should improve and the company shouldn’t see the same share losses with Tesla (TSLA), the company is still highly reliant on Tesla as a customer in power, and rivals like Infineon, NXP, and Renesas have been stepping up their game in MCUs and ADAS products.

While the outlook for STM’s SiC power may be mixed in auto, I’m more confident about the near-term outlook in areas like industrial and DCs, particularly with industrial automation and electrification recovering from sharp recent declines.

New content wins should also help results in 2026. STMicro has won more imaging content on new Apple phones, and there are rumors that the company also won the LiDAR sensor business for the iPhone 18 from Sony (SONY). The company has also been securing wins for other sensor products and its BiCMOS offerings in areas like autos, DCs, and satellites.

Higher Expectations For DC And Physical AI Markets Add To The Story

While a recovery in SiC power and increased iPhone content, as well as underlying recoveries in auto and industrial end-markets, would be enough to drive a stronger second half in 2026, management’s recent commentary on its opportunities in the data center and physical AI (robotics in particular) has boosted confidence in the longer-term secular growth story.

Earlier in March, management hosted a call and presentation on its DC opportunities and ambitions, sizing a $200-$250 million addressable market for every incremental gigawatt of DC capacity. With management expecting over 20% compound annual growth in hyperscaler capex from 2024 to 2030, that works out to over $500 million in 2026 and over $1 billion in 2027.

STMicro is looking to play in both the power and connectivity segments of data centers. For power, management expects the move to 800V to create new opportunities for the company, particularly for SiC and GaN architectures. I believe this is true in principle, but I believe Infineon is better placed at the outset of this opportunity, and STMicro will have to demonstrate that it can transfer capabilities built largely for the auto market to the DC power opportunity.

The connectivity side looks less risky to me, given that the company already has a strong position in areas like BiCMOS electronic ICs (or EICs), microcontrollers, and photonics ICs. With more DCs moving from copper cabling to optical and STMicro ramping up its capabilities and capacity (including a plan to eventually offer co-packaged optics), this could ultimately be the stronger part of this business.

Underlying this opportunity, the company announced a multiyear collaboration with Amazon’s (AMZN) AWS that will see the company provide a wide range of products for AWS DCs, including high-performance mixed-signal processors, advanced microcontrollers, and analog and power ICs. As part of the deal, STMicro is giving Amazon warrants equivalent to almost 25 million shares that will vest over several years—effectively giving Amazon a price discount to secure share/volumes.

Roughly a week later, management hosted another call outlining the company’s leverage in humanoid robotics. Given existing strengths in power, sensing, microcontrollers, and discretes, this is a natural market for STMicro, and I wasn’t all that surprised that the company sized the opportunity at roughly $600 per unit (in line with the $500 quoted by Infineon), or around 20% to 33% of the total COGS of a humanoid robot based on third-party COGS/bill of material estimates.

It’s going to take time for the robotics opportunity to materialize, but STM’s deep experience and strong market positions across power, microcontrollers, and sensing give me more confidence in their ability to secure a good share here in the future. I’d also note that a lot of these components will have broad applicability beyond just humanoid robotics, as many physical AI edge devices and simpler industrial robots will need many of the same components.

The Outlook

For at least the near term, management seems to have successfully redirected the conversation around STMicro from concerns about shaky near-term auto and industrial markets, overreliance on key customers like Apple and Tesla, MCU share losses, and overcapacity to an impending broad-based recovery, share/socket wins, and significant revenue opportunities in DCs and physical AI.

I’d also note that management has been making responsible choices recently regarding capex and capacity. While I do have some concerns that success in the DC and robotics ventures would require more capacity than management currently suggests, capex is under control now, and management just announced that it had started the first deliveries of fully Chinese-produced MCUs for Chinese customers (using Hua Hong as a manufacturing partner).

Between a generally positive reporting cycle for industrial companies, tightening channel inventories, and upgraded DC expectations, my numbers for STMicro are higher now than before, with my 2026 revenue expectation about 6% higher, my 2027 number about 5% higher, and my 2028 number about 4% higher. My long-term revenue growth rate goes from around 6% to 8%, but some of that is also the change in the base year; apples-to-apples, the changes in my modeling assumptions lead to about 50bp higher long-term revenue growth than before.

I haven’t made especially meaningful changes to my margins or cash flow expectations, and I’m still expecting low double-digit operating margins in 2027. Recoveries in areas like SiC power should have a disproportionate positive impact on margins, but I’m also a bit cautious about the pricing of new business wins. Either way, simply seeing higher volumes will have a positive impact on margins in the coming quarters and years.

I’m still expecting low double-digit adjusted free cash flow margins over the long term, and that supports a low double-digit long-term annualized total expected return. Likewise, an operating margin around 12% would still support a 2.45x revenue multiple on FY’27 revenue; discounted back, that gets me to a mid-$30’s fair value today. As was the case before, every incremental point of operating margin is worth about $2/share in additional fair value.

The Bottom Line

In retrospect, it was wrong for me to go “Neutral” on these shares in December on concerns that the Street would be overly concerned about the risks of a recovery more weighted to the second half of 2026. Since then, companies in STMicro’s addressed markets have reported generally positive results and guidance, and management has certainly helped by talking up the opportunities in growth markets like DCs and robotics.

I still see risks here, including the risk of a broad macroeconomic slowdown, further MCU share losses, more intense competition in established auto and industrial markets, and more competition than expected in growth opportunities like DC power and robotics. That said, I also see double-digit return potential, demand coming off a cyclical low, and a management team that has done a better job in recent years than they’ve gotten credit for. With that, I do think these shares are worth considering even after this rebound. I upgrade shares to a "Buy."
